title: Dog Soft Fatty Lump on Ribcage: Lipomas and Veterinary Triage
meta_desc: Learn about soft fatty lumps (lipomas) on your dog's ribcage, urgency levels, and why documenting the growth with photos is essential for care.
content_html:
What is a soft fatty lump?

A soft, moveable, and painless lump under your dog's skin—particularly along the ribcage—is most commonly a lipoma. Lipomas are benign (non-cancerous) tumors composed entirely of fat cells. They are very common in middle-aged and senior dogs. While they are typically harmless, they can occasionally grow large enough to cause discomfort or restrict movement.

Is it an emergency?

Urgency Level: Low. In most cases, a fatty lump on the ribs is not an emergency. You should, however, monitor the site closely. If you notice the lump growing rapidly, changing texture from soft to firm, or if the skin over the lump becomes red or ulcerated, you should schedule a routine veterinary appointment for a physical exam and a fine-needle aspirate to confirm the diagnosis.

Why a photo helps triage

Providing a photo (or a series of photos) to your veterinarian is an excellent way to triage the growth. Visual documentation allows the vet to assess the location and surface appearance of the lump. More importantly, keeping a photo log over time helps you and your vet determine the exact rate of growth, which is a critical factor in deciding whether surgical removal is necessary.

faqs.question: Can a lipoma turn into cancer?
faqs.answer: True lipomas are benign and do not turn into malignant cancer, but they can be difficult to distinguish from more serious growths without a veterinary test called a fine-needle aspirate.

faqs.question: Should I try to squeeze the lump?
faqs.answer: No, you should never squeeze or attempt to 'pop' a lump on your dog. This can cause inflammation, pain, or a secondary infection if the lump is not what it appears to be.

faqs.question: Are certain breeds more prone to fatty lumps?
faqs.answer: Yes, while any dog can develop them, breeds like Labrador Retrievers, Weimaraners, and Doberman Pinschers are statistically more likely to develop lipomas as they age.
